coredata.dc:title: The emerging development of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: Synthesis methods, influencing factors and applications
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Qiang
Li, Ning
Lu, Kangzhi
Dong, Zhanhua
Yang, Yuewei
coredata.dc:description: A novel family of zero-dimensional photoluminescent nanoscale materials called carbon dots has a wide range of possible applications and great optical and electrochemical characteristics. A potent and adaptable method that can further enhance the internal or surface structure of carbon dots is nitrogen and sulfur co-doping. In this review, we update the roadmap for nitrogen and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) from various synthesis techniques to determine the factors that influence their brightness. At the same time, the uses of N, S-CDs in nanoprobes, photoelectric devices, biomedicine, food safety detection, environmental quality monitoring, catalysis and information encryption is presented. We anticipate a more in-depth study to uncover the potential of N, S-CDs as well as their future development prospects.

head.ce:title: The emerging development of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots: Synthesis methods, influencing factors and applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qufu Normal University, Rizhao, Shandong, 276826, China

Corresponding author. Qufu Normal University, Rizhao, Shandong, 276826, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel family of zero-dimensional photoluminescent nanoscale materials called carbon dots has a wide range of possible applications and great optical and electrochemical characteristics. A potent and adaptable method that can further enhance the internal or surface structure of carbon dots is nitrogen and sulfur co-doping. In this review, we update the roadmap for nitrogen and sulfur-co-doped carbon dots (N, S-CDs) from various synthesis techniques to determine the factors that influence their brightness. At the same time, the uses of N, S-CDs in nanoprobes, photoelectric devices, biomedicine, food safety detection, environmental quality monitoring, catalysis and information encryption is presented. We anticipate a more in-depth study to uncover the potential of N, S-CDs as well as their future development prospects.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots are zero-dimensional nanoluminescent materials that have attracted extensive research attention due to their special fluorescent luminescence properties, biocompatibility, and easy synthesis [1–3]. In 2004, Xu et al. introduced a carbon dot electrophoresis technique for purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes generated from arc discharge flue gas. This study resulted in the discovery of two novel nanomaterials. Among them are mixtures of fluorescent nanoparticles derived from single-walled carbon nanotubes [4]. However, to obtain carbon dots with better performance, an increasing number of strategies to improve performance have beenproposed. Doping is a commonly used methods for improving the properties of carbon dots because it can further improve the properties of carbon dots to a certain extent, such as increacing water solubility, wider luminescence range, higher stability and better dispersion. An increasing number of studies on doped carbon dots have appeared [5–7]. Doped carbon dots are considered to have great research potential in various fields due to their various excellent properties, such as environmental monitoring, food safety, and biomedicine, catalysis, photoelectric devices and information encryption, which have specific applications. The photoluminescence property of carbon dots is not only determined by the size but also depends on its surface state. It is expected that the fluorescence emission behavior of carbon quantum dots can be regulated by doping different elements and groups on the surface of carbon dots and exist on the surface of carbon dots in different forms of groups. Atomic doping introduces additional energy levels into the energy band structure of carbon dots, and these additional energy levels can affect the optical absorption, fluorescence emission, and other properties of carbon dots. For example, doping of nitrogen, sulfur and other elements can adjust the energy level structure of carbon dots, thus improving their photocatalytic activity and fluorescence performance. The optical properties of carbon dots are affected by their size, morphology and structure. Atomic doping modulates the optical properties of carbon dots, such as the position and intensity of the absorption spectrum and the wavelength of the fluorescence emission [8]. This synergistic effect can further adjust the band gap of the carbon dots and realize the precise control of the optical properties of the carbon dots. In addition, different functional groups can specifically recognize certain heavy metal ions, which can be used as sensors for heavy metal ion detection with high selectivity, high sensitivity and excellent anti-interference ability. As research progresses, carbon dots exhibit some drawbacks. Therefore, the advanced research team further studied the modification and functionalization of carbon dots by doping heteroatoms into the carbon dots. Usually, to further improve the specific properties of carbon dots, a co-doping strategy is introduced. After searching a large number of excellent studies, it was found that nitrogen-sulfur co-doping is more common. Nitrogen atoms are typical dopant atoms, with a similar size to carbon atoms. Nitrogen doping is the most frequent way of doping carbon dots with heteroatoms because they can improve the photoluminescence properties of carbon dots [9–15]. Zhang et al. reported a nitrogen-doped highly fluorescent carbon quantum dot, and the N-doped carbon dots exhibited strong blue emission, which was due to the fluorescence-enhancing effect of N atoms introduced into the carbon dots [13]. In addition to nitrogen doping, sulfur doping has also attracted much attention [8,16–22]. The introduction of sulfur into the carbon dots makes the carbon dots more stable and can also change the electronic structure of the carbon dots to provide energy for photoexcited electron capture during the photoluminescence process of the carbon dots, or to increase the emission trap states, which is beneficial to the photoluminescence emission of carbon dots. Hu et al. used waste frying oil as a raw material to prepare pH-sensitive luminescent sulfur-doped carbon dots in one step, and the prepared carbon dots showed excellent photostability [18].

Although nitrogen atom doping and sulfur atom doping have advantages, there are still certain limitations. Their single-atom-doped light-emitting regions are more limited, mostly in the blue‒green range. Although the doping of nitrogen atoms can greatly improve the emission efficiency of carbon dots, the content of nitrogen and the formation of C–N and CN are the keys to improving the photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) [23]. Although the stability of doping of sulfur atoms is good, their atomic doping rate is low, and no additional effects can be obtained. However, the co-doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms can effectively improve these limitations, and the additional doping of sulfur can increase the doping concentration of nitrogen [8]. The light-emitting area is no longer limited, and dual-emission or even multicolor light can be achieved. Therefore, N, S-CDs have been widely studied [24–47]. The co-doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms can reduce the most effective gap of the energy band, and its material is superior to carbon dots, with higher photogenerated current capability, higher absorption coefficient and longer absorption wavelength, making it excellent for photovoltaic application candidates [39]. Zhou's team used a one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesize special N, S-CDs. The as-prepared carbon dots not only had high photoluminescence intensity and quantum yield, but also had unique dual emission (blue and yellow) and excitation-independent characteristics under single excitation. A control experiment was also set up to prove that the unique properties of N, S-CDs are due to the co-doping effect of S and N atoms, which provides a favorable method for the development of solid-state lighting materials [48]. Carbon dots doped with nitrogen heteroatoms generally have low efficiency in the red fluorescence region. In view of this, Qin's team used tetrahydrofuran as a solvent to prepare multicolor emission N, S-CDs by a one-step solvothermal method, and the prepared carbon dots showed different fluorescence emission colors from blue to yellow‒red, covering almost the entire visible light spectrum. As a sensor probe, it was used to monitor metal ions in industrial pollutants [49]. The luminous color richness is realized under nitrogen and sulfur co-doping. A comparison of the chromatographic distribution bands of single nitrogen doping, single sulfur doping and nitrogen sulfur co-doping is summarized in Fig. 1 [18,31,33,47,48,50–58].

Numerous studies on carbon dots have been devoted to improving their characteristics and enhancing their optical and electrochemical properties. Nitrogen-sulfur atoms are doped in the carbon dots to adjust their surface state or electronic structure, so the performance of the carbon dots is improved by co-doping the carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur. Many reviews devoted to the doping modification of carbon dots, and there are many reviews on doped carbon dots [7,59,60], but no review on nitrogen-sulfur co-doping has been proposed. Compared with single nitrogen and single sulfur doping, we will focus on the synthetic strategies, fluorescence properties, and research potentials in various fields based on a series of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: At present, there are various literature reports on the synthesis strategies of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots. These synthesis methods mainly include several categories: hydrothermal or solvothermal methods and microwave. Here, the well-known hydrothermal method is the main synthetic method in the solvothermal category, which will be explained in detail later. In addition, there are some other synthetic methods such as pyrolysis, carbonization, solid-phase heat treatment and ultrasonic treatment.

Because of its pervasiveness, carbon is a source that has received much attention and investigation due to the variety of sources that are readily available. Several widely accessible basic materials are employed as precursors to create co-doped carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur using different techniques. In 2013, Sun et al. reported that carbonization and corrosion of hair with concentrated sulfuric acid resulted in tunable luminescence N, S-CDs of materials used as bioimaging and biomarkers [61]. In 2018, Dang's team used a single-pot solid-state method to heat a solid mixture of caffeine, ammonium persulfate, and urea to obtain highly photoluminescent nitrogen-sulfur-co-doped carbon dots used as fluorescent probes for silver ions [62]. In 2019, Das's team used oxidative pyrolysis of waste garlic peels to prepare excellent carbon dots which were applied to nanocomposite films due to their outstanding optical stability and resistance to photobleaching. The conversion of waste materials into useful materials provides a theoretical basis [63].

In addition to natural substances, the source of carbon dots can also be obtained from chemicals, and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots are synthesized through a specific synthesis strategy. In 2013, Dong et al. reported that carbon dots synthesized by citric acid and l-cysteine showed high photoluminescence quantum yield and emission independent of excitation wavelength behavior has important applications in the field of bioimaging [29]. In 2015, Li's team used citric acid and dithioacetamide as raw materials to synthesize N, S-CDs in one step using a microwave-assisted method. Because the obtained carbon dots had good sensitivity to mercury ions, they were applied to lake water. Detection of metal ions in tap water [35]. In 2017, Zhou et al. reported a unique nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dot synthesized by the solvothermal method. This type of carbon dot was synthesized by m-phenylenediamine, sulfuric acid, and ethanol with unique luminescence features, providing a method for the development of solid-state lighting materials [48]. There are a number of synthesis methods for the synthesis of N, S-CDs, each of which corresponds to different reaction conditions, and correspondingly produces carbon dots with different properties, which have corresponding applications in different fields. These methods mainly include hydrothermal or solvothermal methods, microwave-assisted synthesis, pyrolysis, solid-state synthesis, and ultrasonic treatment. In this chapter, we will introduce these synthetic methods in detail, including different synthetic raw materials and different preparation methods.

The raw materials used to synthesize N, S-CDs can be obtained in a wide range. They can be obtained from biomass or prepared with chemicals as precursors. Each has its own advantages. This section will start with biomass to describe some synthetic methods of using biomass as raw material. The sources of biomass are very wide, ranging from ordinary plants, leaves and fruits of plants, and some biological wastes seen in daily life. The preparation of doped carbon dots using biomass as a raw material usually has excellent water solubility. In addition, there are various properties that will make carbon dots have great application value in many fields. These advantageous biomasses, supplemented by appropriate and advanced synthesis strategies, will improve the performance of carbon dots to a certain extent, making them have great application potential. Table 1 lists the properties of N, S-CDs using biomass as the precursor.

The raw materials of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots can be obtained in nature and are reproducible. In the hydrothermal process, biomass is used as a raw material to heat fracture its specific functional groups, so that the surface of the prepared N, S-CDs has water-soluble functional groups, thus N, S-CDs have excellent water solubility, and the prepared carbon dot also has low toxicity. Biomass has a wide range of sources, such as plants [67] and fruits [64,70], which can be found everywhere in nature. Carbohydrates [73] and polysaccharide derivatives [76] are also sources. In addition, biological waste can also be used as a raw material to prepare carbon dots. These biological wastes [78] are treated by a hydrothermal process to obtain the required carbon dots while reducing environmental pollution. Therefore, the hydrothermal process using biomass as raw material is also environmentally friendly. The hydrothermal method using biomass as raw material is also known as the traditional hydrothermal process. Diversified biomass makes the preparation of carbon dots diversified.

Plants and fruits . Plants or some foods such as onion, ginger, and garlic, are the most common sources of biomass. Using them as raw materials to prepare N, S-CDs will effectively improve the luminescence stability of N, S-CDs, and the prepared carbon dots are generally smaller in size. Sun et al. used garlic as a green synthetic material to synthesize doped carbon dots with high luminescence stability, which showed excellent stability (Fig. 2 a) in a wide pH range and high NaCl concentration [23]. It is worth noting that the prepared carbon dots can be applied to complex and harsh conditions. The luminescence stability of carbon dots will provide favorable support for the detection of special substances. Hu's team used garlic and ginger to prepare nitrogen-sulfur co-doped biological mass dots using garlic and ginger (Fig. 2b), which emit strong green fluorescence as a fluorescent nanometer based on the internal filter effect (IFE) [68]. The probe was used to detect thrombin, which provides a new strategy for enzyme activity determination using biomass co-doped carbon quantum dots.

Carbohydrates and polysaccharide derivatives
. Carbon dots prepared with carbohydrates and their derivatives as precursors have strong sensitivity. This feature provides a certain theoretical basis for sensor design. Fu's team prepared N, S-CDs using glucose as a raw material, sodium sulfite as a doping source, and sulfur-containing groups to find that they can passivate the surface of carbon dots [74]. Under the influence of the solvent hydroxyl group, they found that the carbon dots showed pH sensitive fluorescence characteristics. Zhang's research team used glucose as a carbon source to prepare new bifunctional N, S-CDs, which were used for the temperature and erlotinib detection of fluorescent nanoprobes [72]. In addition, Liu et al. found that N, S-CDs prepared from polysaccharide derivatives had a larger particle size than ordinary carbon dots [76]. They believed that the formation of large spheres may be related to heteroatom doping.

Biowaste. In addition to the above sources, biowaste can also be used as a raw material, and the carbon dots prepared by it are used to make the carbon dots more biocompatible and have sufficient fluorescent activity. At the same time, biowaste is used for preparation, which improves the utilization rate of waste and reduces pollution. Sharma et al. used rose petals to synthesize nitrogen and sulfur co-doped rose carbon dots (N–S@RCD) for dual-fluorescence multianalyte sensing [77]. In addition, Li's group used harmful green algae blooms to prepare highly fluorescently active N, S-CDs for multifunctional fluorescent probe imaging, realizing the reuse of biological waste and transforming it into multifunctional carbon dots [78]. This will have important significance for protecting the environment.

Based on the above discussion, the hydrothermal method is a synthesis method of N, S-CDs using biomass as a raw material. In addition, there are a small number of other synthetic methods reported in the literature. Biomass is a carbon source, or biomass itself contains nitrogen and sulfur elements. Finally, the target doped carbon dots are obtained.

Microwave-assisted synthesis of N, S-CDs is a green and economical synthesis method. The carbon dots synthesized by this method have excellent performance and a high recovery rate, which is of great significance for the development of "carbon neutrality" under low-carbon development. By placing the biomass in a microwave oven, some green plants are even heated by microwave radiation due to their special structure, the mixture warms up rapidly and the characteristic structure is used to synthesize carbon dots with special luminescent behavior. Chatzimitakos et al. covered the successful synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur-doped carbon dots with strong fluorescent properties using human nails as the raw material by a microwave-assisted method in their 2018 study. The resulting carbon dots were found to have a remarkably small size distribution of approximately 2 nm [81]. In terms of fluorescence emission performance, it exhibited bright blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield and ultralow detection limit (0.1 nm). Controlling environmental pollution still faces enormous challenges, such as frequent algal blooms, which can damage aquatic ecosystems, release harmful substances, and affect the biodiversity of aquatic ecosystems. Zhang et al. covered a nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dot for microwave treatment of green algae crude oil [80]. This initiative can both control algal bloom pollution and algal populations, and successfully use these pollutants for human benefits. The produced carbon dots had high solubility and excellent biocompatibility and successfully applied it to bioimaging. Based on the above case, we can understand that microwave-assisted synthesis is a green synthesis method with great research potential for the preparation of N, S-CDs. Microwave-assisted synthesis has broad prospects for the development of low-carbon life because of its green environmental protection. This means that there will be much research leaning towards this green synthetic approach to preparations for human needs.

Pyrolytic carbonization and oxidation treatments can affect the structure of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped biomass carbon dots, including surface morphology, pore structure, and crystal structure. These structural changes will directly affect the specific surface area, pore distribution, and electrical conductivity and other properties of the carbon dots. And it may change the distribution state and chemical environment of nitrogen and sulfur elements in the carbon dots, which in turn affects their optical properties. In 2017, Song's research team reported a highly water-soluble N, S-CDs prepared by carbonizing casein with concentrated sulfuric acid. The prepared N, S-CDs had higher stability [88]. In 2019, Romero et al. used broccoli extract as a green carbon source and prepared N, S-CDs by photochemical oxidation. Infrared or IR low-energy light was converted to 415–500 nm UV–Vis high-energy light, and the prepared N, S-CDs had a fluorescence quantum yield of 22%. One of the main advantages of photochemical oxidation is that the synthesis time is shorter than that of the traditional hydrothermal method [87]. In 2020, Hu et al. prepared green and environmentally friendly N, S-CDs from discarded green tea leaves. Such carbon dots were prepared by a combination of high-temperature pyrolysis and concentrated sulfuric acid carbonization. The high photoluminescence quantum yield and stability, but to a certain extent temperature sensitivity, made it suitable for use as a temperature sensor [85]. Synthetic methods such as high-temperature pyrolysis carbonization and photochemical oxidation can improve the stability of carbon dots to a certain extent and can also be used in sensors with selective detection that are sensitive to certain environmental conditions.

Based on the discussion above, a large number of research groups have adopted specific synthetic methods for the preparation of carbon dots from biomass, including hydrothermal or solvothermal methods, microwave-assisted pyrolysis, concentrated sulfuric acid carbonization and photochemical oxidation, to prepare N, S-CDs. The doping of nitrogen and sulfur endows carbon dots with some better properties than undoped carbon dots (referred to as O-CDs), including photoluminescence properties, stability, water solubility, and biocompatibility. These discussions show that the preparation of N, S-CDs from biomass has certain theoretical guiding significance for the current research on carbon neutralization and doping of carbon dots.

Numerous studies have shown that in addition to using biomass as a carbon source. N, S-CDs can also be prepared through specific synthesis steps from non-biomass, which has, some chemicals containing carbon, nitrogen, and sulfur. N, S-CDs synthesized by green chemical synthesis have the advantages of high purity and are easily controlled. The controllable synthesis and high purity of carbon dots means that the use of chemicals to synthesize N, S-CDs can improve the quality of N, S-CDs to a certain extent. Additionally, green chemical synthesis can also improve the photobleaching resistance of carbon dots. In addition, green chemical synthesis generally produces higher wavelengths. There are many methods for the preparation of N, S-CDs by chemical synthesis, such as solvothermal treatment, microwave-assisted synthesis, the reflux method and ultrasonic treatment. Some of them use a single precursor and some use multiple precursors, which will be discussed in detail below.

Many studies have used a single precursor to prepare N, S-CDs, since a single precursor can simultaneously provide a carbon source, a nitrogen source, and a sulfur source, which is simple and easy to implement. In these studies, the methods for preparing N, S-CDs from a single precursor are mainly hydrothermal or solvothermal. Table 2 lists the properties of N, S-CDs derived from a single precursor. The hydrothermal or solvothermal synthesis of N, S-CDs with a single substance as a precursor has a certain simplicity and ease of manipulation.

Heparin sodium is an anticoagulant chemical that also contains carbon, nitrogen, sulfur, oxygen, and hydrogen elements. Sun et al. reported the one-step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon using heparin sodium as a single precursor in 2015. The resulting carbon dots demonstrated small particle size and excitation-dependent blue‒green fluorescence. Furthermore, the N, S-CDs exhibited good biocompatibility and low toxicity, making them suitable for live cell imaging and ion optional purposes [89]. In 2020, Deng's research group studied N, S-CDs synthesized via one-step solvothermal synthesis using sulfamic acid as a single raw material. The fluorescence of the prepared carbon dots showed excitation independence, good dispersion and narrow distribution size and had been successfully used for ion detection because of its excellent selectivity [90]. It can be concluded that the hydrothermal or solvothermal synthesis of N, S-CDs with a single chemical can improve the performance of carbon dots to a certain extent, and it is uncomplicated and controllable in the research routing and has the characteristics of higher relative purity and better dispersion.

Apart from the abovementioned synthesis strategies of a single precursor, there are a number of research teams that synthesize N, S-CDs from a variety of raw materials, including the preparation of citric acid‒based N, S-CDs, citrate-based N, S-CDs and other multiple precursors. Compared with a single precursor, the synthesis efficiency of multicomponent precursor is higher, because the interaction of multiple precursors during the synthesis process can regulate the morphology and properties of carbon dots, and the specific functional groups can increase the fluorescence quantum yield and even improve the stability of carbon dots. Table 3 shows properties of N, S-CDs derived from multiple precursors.

Citric Acid-Based Synthesis. The most frequent source of carbon is citric acid. It is a special chemical that can be used to create carbon nanodots with strong fluorescence. It also includes three reactive carboxyl groups, allowing for adequate passivation, carbonization, and dehydration. Better water-soluble nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots may be produced by using citric acid and citrates as carbon sources. Citric acid is more easily decomposed at high temperatures and is suitable for use when synthesizing carbon dots at high temperatures. If it is necessary to synthesize carbon dots under high temperature conditions, citric acid can be selected as a precursor. Citrate-based carbon dots can be prepared by reacting with or without various passivation agents or dopants based on small organic molecules, including urea, ethylenediamine (EDA) and amino acids. Due to its advantages, such as easy synthesis, perfect biocompatibility and good fluorescence properties, the preparation of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with citric acid and citrate has enormous potential in biomedical applications. These high-quality multiple materials are precisely used as precursors to prepare N, S-CDs by many advanced methods, including hydrothermal methods and microblogging-assisted methods which can greatly improve various performances.

Hydrothermal. The hydrothermal method is an eco-friendly synthetic route that is direct, low cost, green and highly efficient. The hydrothermal synthesis of N, S-CDs based on citric acid was first studied in 2013 [29]. l-cysteine is a common amino acid in organisms that can provide nitrogen (amino) and sulfur (thiol) at the same time. In 2015, Xue's research group prepared N, S-CDs using citric acid and l-cysteine as raw materials by a one-step hydrothermal method. The as-prepared carbon dots exhibited ion selectivity and high sensitivity [99]. Thiamine (VB1) is a well-known vitamin, often in the form of hydrochloride, also known as thiamine hydrochloride. In 2018, Liao's research team prepared N, S-CDs with high optical and chemical stability using citric acid and thiamine hydrochloride (Fig. 3 a) by a one-step hydrothermal method [100]. In the hydrothermal synthesis of N, S-CDs, although most of them are one-step syntheses, some studies have recently reported two-step syntheses. The highlight of the two-step hydrothermal method is that the nitrogen and sulfur doped in the carbon dots can be directionally selected, and the properties of the carbon dots are capable of being gradually improved by postprocessing. In 2021, Liu et al. demonstrated a two-step hydrothermal synthesis of N, S-CDs using citric acid and sulfamic acid as raw materials. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a passivating agent improved the fluorescence performance (Fig. 3bi). Under the same emission band, it was found that the dispersed N, S-CDs had only one excitation band, while the aggregated N, S-CDs had two excitation bands (Fig. 3bii), so a dual excitation ratio fluorescence sensor was produced. The sensor selectively detected chlorogenic acid (CGA) with high water solubility and stability [101]. There are many forms of hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots, and the final goal is to successfully dope nitrogen and sulfur into carbon dots.

Solvothermal. Solvothermal treatment has begun to appear in people's cognition. With the development of research, organic matter is used as the reaction solvent, the raw materials are placed in the reaction solvent to obtain better fluorescence properties of carbon dots, and the physical and chemical properties of carbon dots can be precisely controlled by controlling the type of solvent [102]. In a closed container (stainless steel autoclave), it can effectively prevent the volatilization of toxic substances. Moreover, it can be found in many studies that N, S-CDs prepared by the solvothermal method produce a variety of special luminescence behaviors, with relatively longer wavelengths (redshifted) or dual emission. Most of the red emission is used in the medical field. The reason is that red light can penetrate the biological body without interference fromtissue autofluorescence. Thus, these results prove that solvothermal synthesis is a convenient and efficient synthesis method. Following the hydrothermal synthesis of N, S-CDs based on citric acid, there have been many articles on solvothermal methods. In 2018, Qie et al. produced N, S-CDs from thiourea citrate via the most familiar solvothermal method, and the as-obtained carbon dots had excellent photoluminescence properties, exhibiting red light emission behavior, high stability, and unique sensitivity for iron ions [103]. In 2020, Wen's group reported the solvothermal synthesis of N, S-CDs using citric acid, ammonium 1-pyrrolidinedithiocarbamate (A1P) and theophylline (TH) as the starting materials. By adjusting the molar ratio of reactants and reaction temperature, and adding different solvents, the optimal emission wavelength of carbon dots changed from blue light emission to yellow photoluminescence [104]. Multicolor luminescence improves the optoelectronic properties of carbon dots and has important application significance in optoelectronics. In addition, Qin et al. also prepared N, S-CDs with CA, guanidine thiocyanate (GITC) and tetrahydrofuran (THF) under solvothermal conditions. The properties of the luminescent carbon dots prepared with the reaction temperature changed, showing multicolor luminescence [49]. In addition, the synthetic reaction based on N,S-CDs can be assisted by other factors to continue to achieve N,S-CDs with better properties, and the production of solid red light carbon dots under solvothermal conditions using citric acid as a precursor by Ding et al. has broadened the way for the study of N,S-CDs [105]. The solvothermal method has been broadly studied for its preparation of polychromatic luminescent carbon dots.
Microwave-assisted synthesis. Microwave-assisted synthesis is a fast, environmentally friendly, uniformly heated, and economical synthesis route. The carbon dots prepared by microwaves from

Natural sources need to be purified later. In contrast, precursors based on citric acid are prepared by microwave-assisted synthesis. The high recovery rate of carbon dots can also make the carbon dots have higher purity and can be used directly. Closed microwave-assisted synthesis is a type of precise temperature control in an enclosed system that can accomplish high-temperature carbonization, significantly reduce reaction time, and increase the efficiency of preparing carbon dots in one pot. The closed microwave-assisted synthesis can achieve carbonization at high temperature under the premise of precise temperature control and a safe pressure relief system, which greatly shortens the reaction time and makes the preparation of carbon dots more efficient; thus, it can be modified by chemical adjustment of one-pot synthesis. The closed microwave-assisted synthesis includes microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis and microwave-assisted solid-phase synthesis. In 2015, Wang et al. reported a method for the rapid synthesis of N, S-CDs using citric acid and dithiooxamide as raw materials using microwave-assisted hydrothermal treatment, and the reaction time was only 2.5 min. The synthesized carbon dots had high efficiency blue fluorescence, tiny size and high selectivity and sensitivity (Fig. 4 a) [106]. In 2017, Liu's team demonstrated the synthesis of N, S-CDs with both high fluorescence quantum yield and enzyme-mimicking catalytic activity. The carbon dots were synthesized by microwave-assisted solid-phase pyrolysis. Citric acid monohydrate and thiourea as precursors were synthesized in only 50 s [107]. Numerous studies have confirmed that microwave-assisted synthesis is a simple, green, fast, and efficient method for the preparation of doped carbon dots with extremely short reaction times.

An intelligent alternative to closed microwave synthesis is open microwave assisted synthesis, which simply takes the radiation frequency, power output, and time into account. The microwave time can also be increased to produce carbonization. The precise temperature of the closed microwave-assisted synthesis needs to be considered due to its closedness. Controlled and safe pressure relief systems andrelated studies have demonstrated that N, S-CDs synthesized by open microwaves have very good recovery rates. Open microwave-assisted synthesis methods include one-step microwave-assisted synthesis [108,109], microwave-assisted pyrolysis [110] and microwave-assisted oil bath [111]. In 2016, Liu's team used microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid and N-acetyl-l-cysteine to prepare N, S-CDs in just 8 min. The as-prepared doped carbon dots were selective for special metal ions, while the corresponding C–S, amino CO–N, CON–H, CN and CO improved the stability of the target carbon dots in water and hydrophilicity (Fig. 4b) [110]. In addition, Zhou's team recently demonstrated a new preparation method using citric acid and glutathione as precursors, to synthesize doped carbon dots in 1 h under microwave-assisted oil bath condition. The synthesized carbon dots were sparsely dispersed and had good water solubility (Fig. 4c) [111].

Citrate-Based Synthesis . Sodium citrate and ammonium citrate are common citrates in daily life, which are a low-cost and simple organic compounds, and their aqueous solutions have a certain degree of alkalinity and are often used as acid modifiers. Citrate is generally more soluble in water, so it is more convenient for the synthesis of carbon dots in the water phase. According to the actual demand, the appropriate precursors are selected to synthesize carbon dots. If it is necessary to synthesize carbon dots in the water phase, citrate can be selected as a precursor, and the synthesized carbon dots may have relatively better water solubility. In addition, it is also commonly used as a buffer and stabilizer as well as the source of carbon elements in the preparation process of N, S-CDs. The correspondingly prepared carbon dots have excellent water solubility and excellent stability in complex environments. Ammonium citrate is mainly used in industrial sewage treatment and is an excellent penetration aid and dispersant. It is often used as a carbon source and nitrogen source raw material in the research of N, S-CDs. N, S-CDs based on citrate have also been extensively studied.

In research on the synthesis methods of citrate-based N, S-CDs, hydrothermal synthesis [112–114] and microwave-assisted synthesis are mainly used. These methods will also greatly improve their performance to meet the needs of the corresponding ideal.

Hydrothermal synthesis. Hydrothermal synthesis is a common synthesis method for citrate-based doped carbon dots. In 2018, Wang et al. reported a one-step hydrothermal synthesis of N, S-CDs using sodium citrate urea thiourea as a precursor. It had excellent hydrophilicity since many functional groups were introduced into the prepared N, S-CDs [112]. In 2019, Han's group used a simple and efficient hydrothermal method to synthesize N, S-CDs in one step, prepared from sodium citrate and thiourea (Fig. 5 a). The fluorescence strength of the as-obtained carbon dots remained basically unchanged after continuous ultraviolet irradiation for 2 h, indicating that it had very high photoluminescence stability and very good water solubility [114]. In addition, Guo et al. prepared completely water-soluble and blue fluorescent N, S-CDs by a hydrothermal method, which showed significant stability under different ion concentration conditions or pH conditions in recent years (Fig. 5b) [113]. Most of the researchers have shown that the N, S-CDs synthesized by the thermal method of citrate brine have excellent characteristics to a certain extent, which will lay the foundation for the application of N, S-CDs.

Microwave-assisted route. The microwave synthesis of N, S-CDs based on citrate is also a simple and efficient option that is economical and environmentally friendly. Under microwave-assisted conditions, doped carbon dots will exhibit very powerful properties, giving them very high application value. For example, N, S-CDs synthesized by a microwave-assisted route have a high fluorescence quantum yield and excellent fluorescence stability, which has an ephemeral reaction time and some excellent properties that give make the prepared carbon dots a variety of application fields. In 2018, Yang et al. studied the one-step synthesis of N, S-CDs using ammonium citrate and l-cysteine by microwave in only 2.5 min, with a fluorescence quantum yield as high as 64%. The synthesized doped carbon dots showed no significant change in fluorescence intensity under UV light irradiation for 3 h and displayed particularly strong luminescence stability in extreme environments such as high salt or dispersed in ethanol (Fig. 6 a) [115]. Xu's research team synthesized target-doped carbon dots with a fluorescence quantum yield of 54.8% using ammonium citrate and cysteamine hydrochloride as raw materials through microwave-assisted pyrolysis, which had stable luminescence quality and high sensitivity (Fig. 6b) [116]. In 2020, Zhao et al. also reported a low toxicity, high fluorescence, water solubility, stability, and objective quantum yield of N, S-CDs prepared by microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, which were prepared with ammonium citrate tribasic and vitamin B1 [117].

Other multiple precursors. Among the numerous studies, the carbon source of N, S-CDs based on citric acid and citrate can also be provided by other substances, and there are also various toxicity issues. Thus, they have low toxicity and biocompatibility and are better used in multi-faceted detection. Various synthetic methods are involved in the synthesis method using multiple precursors as raw materials, such as the most well-known hydrothermal synthesis [118–130], microwave-assisted route [131–138], solid-phase heat treatment [62,98], reflux method, and the large-scale synthesis of nitrogen by aldol condensation developed by excellent teams in recent years [139,140].

Hydrothermal synthesis. With research on N, S-CDs, the synthesis of N, S-CDs based on other multiple precursors can achieve multicolor emission, which is mostly due to excitation-dependent emission behavior. Most of them are blue (below 500 nm emission) but can also grow to red emission (above 600 nm), with a wide range of emission. In 2017, Cui et al. covered the synthesis of nitrogen-sulfur-doped carbon dots with extremely small particle sizes using acrylic acid and methionine as raw materials by a hydrothermal method and discovered the polychromatic luminescence behavior of the prepared carbon dots, which had good biocompatibility and low toxicity (Fig. 7 a) [120]. In 2020, Khan's research group researched the synthesis of a blue fluorescent N, S-CDs using l-lysine and thiourea as raw materials under hydrothermal conditions (Fig. 7b), with quantum yields as high as 53.19%, high water solubility, independent of pH value, and stability at high ion concentrations [122]. Based on the hydrothermal synthesis of N S-CDs by other precursors, is beneficial to the synthesis of polychromatic carbon dots In addition, Zhu's group reported a new type of red fluorescent carbon dot using p-phenylenediamine and ammonium thiocyanate as precursors using a hydrothermal method, which exhibited strong fluorescence at 618 nm under long excitation (598 nm) [128]. Several studies have demonstrated the excellent luminescence properties and multi-color luminescence properties of N, S-CDs. This special fluorescence of doped carbon dots prepared by the hydrothermal method will provide a certain basis for future research.

Solvothermal synthesis. Apart from the above-stated hydrothermal method, the solvothermal method in other multi-component precursors is a further approach to nitrogen-sulfur doping of carbon dots in past studies. Phenylenediamine is a simple aromatic diaminethat can provide carbon and nitrogen sources, is easily soluble in organic solvents, and is the most frequent precursor. There are three isomers of phenylenediamine, o-phenylenediamine [141,142], m-phenylenediamine [48], and p-phenylenediamine [143,144]. In 2018, Zhang et al. described a one-step solvothermal preparation of N, S-CDs using p-phenylenediamine and cysteamine hydrochloride as raw materials. The as-prepared carbon dots possessed excellent blue‒green fluorescence and monodispersity as well as a narrow size distribution (average particle size less than 5 nm) [143]. Cysteine contains a sulfhydryl group and is the most common nitrogen-sulfur source for the preparation of N, S-CDs. In 2019, Zhang's research team synthesized long wavelength dual-emission (595 nm and 648 nm) N, S-CDs via a one-step solvothermal method using o-phenylenediamine and cysteine as raw materials. The obtained carbon dots had an extremely small size distribution (2–5 nm), and the characterization images demonstrated the successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur [142]. The solvothermal route promotes nitrogen-sulfur doping into carbon dots, making them possess excellent optical properties, which will allow carbon dots to have nonnegligible application potential in optical sensing and biomedicine.

Microwave-assisted synthesis. Based on numerous studies, it can be found that there are various kinds of microwave-assisted synthesis based on other multi-precursors, including the simplest single-pot microwave heating, microwave-assisted pyrolysis, and microwave-assisted hydrothermal methods. In 2016, Duan et al. studied a microwave-assisted one-step synthesis of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots using ethylenediamine as a raw material and sulfamic acid as a surface passivator. The obtained nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots had high performance. Synthesis of doped carbon dots from other multi-component precursors gives carbon dots a variety of high-quality properties such as sensitive applications, fluorescence characteristics, high recovery, sensitivity, and accuracy. In addition, with future human relationships in mind, other multi-precursors also minimize recovery, emit bright blue fluorescence, and have excellent water solubility (Fig. 8 a) [131]. In 2019, Pathak's research team successfully achieved co-doping of nitrogen and sulfur with thiourea and triacetate ethylenediamine (TAE buffer) using microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis (Fig. 8b). The realized N, S-CDs had a little particle size (3.6 ± 0.88 nm), a QY of up to 57% and no toxicity [135]. The microwave-assisted synthesis route enables N, S-CDs to have various properties. The excellent fluorescence properties can be used for monitoring probes, bioimaging, etc. The high recovery rate is conducive to the realization of the concept of carbon-neutral and low-carbon development and has great potential.

Carbonization and solid-phase thermal. In addition, there are a small number of studies introducing other synthesis methods of N, S-CDs, and the synthesis conditions are no longer limited. These synthesis methods can also improve the properties of N, S-CDs and can realize tunable luminescence, enabling large-scale integration of carbon dots and improve electrochemical performance even at ambient temperature. Some special high-temperature processes can dope the carbon dots with the efficiency of N, S atoms, and realize the tunable emission of N, S-CDs. The synthesis of multiple precursor-based N, S-CDs at high temperature has been the previously described for solid-phase heat treatment [62]. In 2017, Song's research team successfully made N, S-CDs using casein as a carbon source and concentrated sulfuric acid carbonization. The carbon dots showed extremely strong stability, such as under different external environmental conditions. It was stable under UV light exposure, different pH values, and high ionic strength [88].

Reflux and sonication. Most nitrogen-sulfur co-doping is performed at high temperature, yet in addition to the pathways that have been mentioned, various molecular fluorophores are generated within carbon dots during low-temperature synthesis. The preparation of N, S-CDs with this method can increase the yield of carbon dots and enable large-scale synthesis of N, S-CDs even at ambient temperature. In 2017, Shi et al. reported a rare method to prepare doped carbon dots, N, S co-doped carbon dots prepared by single-pot reflux treatment of C3N3S3 with ethanediamine at a low temperature (80 °C) with high fluorescence quantum yield (30.4%), strong resistance to photobleaching and good stability (Fig. 9 a) [145]. In the past studies, there are many studies on the preparation of N, S-CDs with considerable fluorescence quantum yield, and there are few reports at room temperature. However, with continuous exploration, in 2021, Li's research team superbly used an efficient aldol condensation method (sodium hydroxide and acetaldehyde) to propose the synthesis of N, S-CDs by ultrasonic treatment at room temperature, enabling large-scale synthesis of doped carbon dots at the low-cost kilogram scale (Fig. 9b) [139]. This is a novel advance in the research of N, S-CDs, which opens the potential application of carbon dots in electrochemistry and provides guidance for further research on N, S-CDs. As mentioned above, the synthesis of N, S-CDs can still be accomplished at low temperature, and functionalized carbon materials can be obtained.

It is clear from the discussion above that there are many ways to prepare N, S-CDs. Chemicals and biomass are the two categories of raw materials. Biomass is typically synthesized using hydrothermal, microwave, pyrolysis, and photochemical oxidation processes. Different synthetic methods using chemicals as raw materials are divided into two categories based on single and multiple precursors. The studied single precursors include aminothiophene, cysteine, thioacetamide, ammonium sulfate nitrate, methionine, heparin sodium, sulfur alcohol hydrochloride, glutathione, and sulfamic acid. The common methods used to obtain these single precursors are hydrothermal and solvothermal. Under the multiple precursor category, it is divided into many categories based on various carbon sources, such as citric acid, citrate, and other multi-precursors. The synthesis process of carbon dots by a single precursor is simple and easy to control the size and structure of carbon dots. However, the synthesis efficiency is low and the yield is limited. Compared with a single precursor, the synthesis of carbon dots by multiple precursors can regulate the morphology and properties of carbon dots, and improve the fluorescence quantum yield and stability of carbon dots. The disadvantages include complex synthesis process, which makes it difficult to control the size and structure of carbon dots. Understanding these will be helpful to select suitable precursors and synthesis strategies for preparing target carbon dots. Different synthesis strategies have different conditions and characteristics. The preparation means of N, S-CDs under these multi-component precursors are various, mainly including the most common hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, microwave-assisted synthesis, carbonization and solid-phase heat treatment at extremely high temperature, and one-pot reflux and sonication. Different qualities may be found in the produced carbon dots. The most popular characteristic is photoluminescence. Numerous research teams have examined the fluorescence characteristics of doped carbon dots. For N, S-CDs, it has opened up numerous, broad opportunities in a variety of sectors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible influencing factors of controlled luminescence
ce:section.ce:para: A broad range of controlled luminescence behaviors, which include excitation-dependent or excitation-independent behavior, up-conversion luminescence and down-conversion luminescence, pH, temperature, concentration-dependent luminescence, and various colors of fluorescence, can arise from changes in the internal structure of N, S-CDs. Different doping positions lead to changes in the energy band structure of carbon dots, which affects the fluorescence emission behavior at different excitation wavelengths. In addition, there are some common luminescence redshifts, which are the result of changes in the internal structure of carbon dots caused by changes in external conditions. In this section, the factors affecting the fluorescence of the N, S-CDs will be analyzed from the perspective of the carbon dot structure and other aspects. At present, some researchers have explained the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties of CDs, which mainly include the following mechanisms: the distribution of different emission sites on the surface (surface state distribution), the size distribution of carbon dots (the existence of different particles), and the carbon dot formation of carbon nuclear.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of the surface state distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The doping of N and S into carbon dots will lead to changes in the distribution of multiple surface states (different types of functional groups), in accordance with the consequences of surface distribution states. The fundamental cause of several emission sites in carbon dots is the binding of multiple functional groups to their surface. The related surface states of O-CDs (origin undoped carbon dots) cause a wide energy level distribution, N doping introduces new surface states, and S atoms are introduced into N. S doping can strengthen the N state and weaken the origin state, which leads to the disappearance of the original surface state, and the complex surface state increases the diversity of excited states of CDs. The doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms makes the carbon dots acquire new surface states, and the surface state distribution of N, S-CDs affects the luminescence mechanism of N, S-CDs to a certain extent. The position and concentration of nitrogen and sulfur doping are important factors affecting the excitation-dependent independent behavior of carbon dots. Functional groups on the surface of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots (e.g., carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, etc.) not only affect their solubility and stability, but also change the electron density and local charge distribution of the carbon dots. Defect sites can act as localized electron traps, affecting the electron complexation process and thus altering the luminescent properties. Surface defects can enhance the luminescence intensity of carbon dots after doping with nitrogen and sulfur elements. In 2019, the N, S-CDs prepared by Zuo et al. exhibited excitation-independent behavior and temperature-dependent blue fluorescence, arguing that the strong PL emission mainly comes from surface-doped sulfur and nitrogen atoms, which are co-doped. The sulfur atoms introduce a completely new surface state to the carbon dots (Fig. 10 a) [164]. Li et al. prepared excitation-dependent N, S-CDs using thiamin nitrate as a single raw material. As the excitation wavelength increases, the emission spectrum peaks showed a significant redshift. They believe that the excitation-dependent luminescence is related to the distribution of surface-emitting trap sites in N, S-CQDs (Fig. 10b) [94]. Other researchers have studied the excitation-independent behavior of N, S-CDs. In addition, some researchers believe that excitation-independent N, S surface states are related to homogeneous N, S surface states, which Han et al. explored by synthesizing excitation-independent N, S-CDs using sodium citrate and thiourea [114]. However, in addition to N, S-CDs surface state distribution will affect its performance and some other factors will restrict its performance.

The fluorescence traits of N, S-CDs may be influenced by their variable size distribution as well as the effect of surface state variability. The excitation-dependent optimum emission wavelength will move in the red direction in the presence of an asymmetric size distribution. Carbon dots with a broad particle size distribution will experience many N and S-CD transitions and emission wavelength variations that are dependent on the excitation wavelength, exhibiting excitation-dependent luminescence phenomena. When the particle size distribution is narrow, the resulting band gap is single, and the transition mode is also single. In addition, when the size distribution of the nitrogen-sulfur doped carbon dots is more uniform, then their HOMO and LUMO energy levels will be more consistent than those of the dots with uneven size distribution. This uniformity helps to obtain a narrower emission spectrum, which improves the performance of optical applications. Under different excitation wavelengths, the same transition is presented, and the emission wavelength does not change with the excitation wavelength, indicating that the excitation has nothing to do with the emission. Cen et al. prepared green excitation-dependent N, S-CDs and elucidated an acceptable mechanism for the redshift of the emission spectrum from the complex excited states of different particle sizes and N, S-CDs. Due to the high doping content of nitrogen and sulfur, functional groups in N, S-CDs increase the carbon interlayer spacing with the presence of heteroatoms, making them have wide dimensions of 10–20 nm (Fig. 11 a) [118]. However, Tang et al. prepared N, S-CDs that had blue fluorescence with no obvious excitation correlation. The prepared carbon dots had a relatively uniform surface state and a small size distribution of 2.80–4.40 nm (Fig. 11b) [126]. In addition, the structure of S, N-CDs can affect the FWHM values, e.g., by modulation of parameters such as doping concentration and degree of graphitization, which can make the fluorescence peaks narrower or wider. Li et al. synthesized N,S/C-dots with high fluorescence quantum yield (FLOY,25%). Compared with ammonia-doped C-dots (N/C-dots) prepared using the same method, the obtained N,S/C-dots are characterized by small particle size and uniform surface states. The half-peak full width of N,S/C-dots (88 nm) is much narrower than that of N/C-dots (110 nm). These can be attributed to the fact that N,S/C -dots have a more homogeneous localized surface state energy band structure and a narrower size distribution than N/C -dots [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Internal structure of the carbon core
ce:section.ce:para: The matching luminescence characteristic is brought on by the N, S-CDs fundamental internal structure. Temperature also has a consequence on crystallinity, and high temperature will accelerate the graphitization process of N and S co-doped carbon quantum dots, which will trigger the fluorescence emission to change to the red. The lattice structure of carbon dots determines the distribution and motion state of their electrons. Doping with nitrogen and sulfur atoms results in a higher degree of graphitization (i.e., an increase in the proportion of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms), which usually leads to better electronic conductivity because the π-electrons are more readily movable in the graphitized structure. This structure may lead to a lower energy gap, making the energy difference between HOMO and LUMO smaller, which affects the luminescence wavelength and intensity. In addition, structural modulation can improve the fluorescence quantum yield, for example, by adjusting the position and concentration of nitrogen and sulfur doping and changing the internal structure to optimize the energy band structure to achieve higher fluorescence quantum yield. High crystallinity can make it much simpler for photogenerated electrons to reach the surface of carbon quantum dots and raise the fluorescence quantum yield. Liu's team prepared double-emitting (yellow and blue) N, S-CDs and found that the internal structure of the carbon core determines the emission band centered at 520 nm (Fig. 12 a) [48]. In addition, Konar et al. synthesized excitation-independent N, S-CDs using tartaric acid and thioacetamide and found that the absorption transitions of the prepared carbon dots were all related to the carbon nucleus [132]. Yang et al. prepared blue and red hydrophobic carbon dots, which emitted red light due to their broad absorption band, which is related to their surface states. They also found that the blue emission is related to their carbonized core structure [56].

Aggregation effect refers to the aggregation of carbon dots in the process of synthesis because of mutual attraction. In the synthesis process of carbon dots, raw materials such as carbon sources and surfactants are usually used to heat or chemically react under certain conditions to form precursor substances of carbon dots. In this process, carbon dots aggregate to form carbon clusters of different shapes and sizes due to the interactions between functional groups on the surface of carbon dots and surfactants. The aggregation effect has an important influence on the synthesis of N, S-CDs. By regulating the aggregation effect, the morphology and structure of the carbon dots can be precisely controlled, which in turn affects the optoelectronic and chemical properties of the carbon dots. Hydrophobic carbon dots (H-CDs) with blue dispersion emission and red aggregation-induced emission were obtained by solvent heat treatment by Yang et al. When water is introduced, the hydrophobic interaction leads to the aggregation of H-CDs. The formation of H-CDs clusters leads to the quenching of blue emission due to the π-π stacking interactions of the carbon nuclei, while the turn-on of red fluorescence is consistent with the aggregation-induced emission phenomenon due to the restriction of intramolecular rotation around the disulfide bonds at the surface (Fig. 13 a). This on-off fluorescence of H-CDs is reversible when the H-CDs powders are completely dissolved [165]. Gopal et al. obtained a scalable dry white luminescent solid material from aqueous dispersions of N, S-CDs by a freeze-drying process without encountering obstacles typically encountered in solid-state CND systems, such as aggregation-induced luminescence quenching (ACQ), low yields, and so on. Aggregation-induced decay in solids is attributed to a reduction in nonradiative relaxation because the interaction between N, S-CNDs and solvent molecules at the center of aggregation is reduced when particles are aggregated together (Fig. 13b) [166].

Co-doped carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur have outstanding electrochemical and fluorescent characteristics, which opens a wide range of possibilities for applications. We detail the specific uses of N–S-CDs in environmental monitoring, food safety testing, and biomedicine, catalysis, photoelectric devices, information encryption (Fig. 14 ).

Industrial wastewater contains many toxic and harmful substances, which directly flow into channels and may seep into groundwater, thereby affecting human health. N, S-CDs specifically detect heavy metal ions, which is very important for environmental detection. Using the cobalt (II) ion (Co2+) as the model, N, S-CDs as the donor, and Co(cys)3 2+ as the acceptor, Chen et al. created a unique metal ion fluorescence sensing model that did not require a particular modification [168]. Numerous studies have shown that N, S-CDs can meet the requirements of environmental monitoring and provide a green way to produce fluorescent probes for detecting heavy metal ions. A one-step microwave reaction was used by Chan's research team to create N, S-CDs, which demonstrated good selectivity for the detection of Fe3+ in water specimens and had a detection limit of 0.16 μM [167].Moreover, onion-based carbon dots were used for the detection of Ag+ in river water and tap water samples. The recoveries ranged from 96.0 to 102.0%, and the precision was below 4.1% [67].

N, S-CDs offer a wide range of useful applications in biomedicine, including drug administration and detection, photodynamic treatment/photothermal therapy, biomacromolecule detection, and bioimaging. The best use of N, S-CDs in this context is their ability to emit long wavelengths of red light, which can prevent self-organized fluorescence and is hence excellent for imaging and healing. Methyl-β-cyclodextrin (Me-β-CD) and methylene blue were the primary raw ingredients employed by Wen's team. The obtained CDs were ideal drug delivery and bioimaging vectors because they were folate sensitive [79]. N, S-CDs can not only detect drugs and biological macromolecules, but also be used for bioimaging and drug delivery in biomedicine to treat diseases. Bao et al. synthesized nitrogen sulfur co-doped carbon dots with low toxicity and targeting as suitable reagents for tumor imaging and photothermal therapy (PTT) and played the biological imaging role of near-infrared fluorescent probes in vivo [155]. Guo et al. prepared an azithromycin (AZM) fluorescent probe with high selectivity and high sensitivity, which could be used for the detection of AZM in tablets and human urine with a high recovery rate [91].Therefore, N, S-CDs have a wide range of applications in medicine, which will be of great help to the treatment of some diseases that cannot be treated due to limitations, such as tumors and other major diseases.

Food additives are mostly synthetic chemical substances, most of which are harmless, but some toxic substances, after being absorbed into the blood and cells by the human body, participate in the chemical reaction process of the human body, interfere with normal body metabolism, and toxic chemicals are produced, thereby increasing the carcinogenicity of food, causing dental caries in children, and affecting children's intellectual development. N, S-CDs can be used not only for biological imaging, but also for food safety detection. The hydrothermal approach was used by Zou's research team to create N, S-CDs, which were then used to create fluorescent sensors for fluazinam, a residual fungicide [130]. The detection of food additives is particularly important in our life. In recent years, many studies have been implemented to detect harmful substances in food. Luo's team covered a switchable sensor based on N, S-CDs to determine ascorbic acid in fruit [169]. In addition, N, S-CDs were created from glutathione by Zhao et al., who also created a fluorescence sensor for the antibacterial drug tetracycline, which reduces the function of the human immune system [97]. Consequently, co-doped carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur offer certain research potential in the domain of food security.

Carbon nanodots with extremely small sizes have been widely studied, in addition to their use as fluorescent probes in environmental monitoring, as carriers for bioimaging and targeted therapy in biomedicine, and for targeted detection of specific substances in food safety. It can also be used in other applications due to its excellent electrochemical performance, such as catalysts. In 2018, Patil et al. first synthesized N, S-CDs, self-assembled on exfoliated multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), and finally prepared N and S co-doped multi-walled carbon nanotubes. As N and S co-doped metal-free carbon catalysts, it exhibited remarkable electrocatalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). It was promising to replace noble metal-based catalysts with metal-free catalysts such as N, S-CDs/C-600 in the cathode compartment [170]. Karaman prepared N, S-CDs from orange peels and fixed them on three-position graphene networks (3D-GNs) to improve their electrocatalytic activity through defect surface effects [171]. In addition, Zhang et al. prepared N, S-CDs and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) composites (called CD/rGO) by a hydrothermal method. The prepared composites all performed better in ORR measurements than their corresponding directly doped graphene oxide counterparts, and the ORR catalytic activity of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped samples was better than that of singly doped samples, demonstrating that N,S-CDs are excellent candidates for a new class of energy conversion catalysts [14].

N, S-CDs have good application prospects in optical devices. Guan et al. prepared N, S-CDs with bright red fluorescence under ultraviolet light by one-step solvothermal method. The WLED developed by N, S-CDs has a considerable CRI, which lays the foundation for making optical devices. Guan et al. co-doped N, S-CDs by one-step solvothermal double emission of nitrogen and sulfur, which fluoresced bright red under ultraviolet light. The WLED developed by N, S-CDs has a considerable CRI, indicating that N, S-CDs have good application prospects in optical devices [33]. Ren et al. synthesized trichromatic CDs with ethanol solvent assistance. The obtained CDs mainly emit blue light, green light and red light, but the photoluminescent quantum yield (FLQY) is generally decreased, which is 54.0% for B-CDs, 11.5% for G-CDs and 8.5% for R-CDs. As the current increases, the extreme value at the same current value means that cd can be applied to WLED devices as a potential fluorescent nanomaterial [172].

In addition, N, S-CDs have good application prospects in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting because of their excellent photoluminescence characteristics. Zhao et al. synthesized N, S-CDs by an electrochemical method, introduced surface sulfur functionalization of the C–S bond, and further improved the luminous intensity by promoting the transition and radiation recombination of S-related excitation electrons. N, S-CDs have been successfully applied to flexible fluorescent films, potential fingerprint detection and information encryption [173]. Liao et al. prepared N, S-CDs with bright orange‒red fluorescence. Because of its high brightness fluorescence and strong water solubility, it is made into fluorescent ink and transparent film, which has broad application potential in anti-counterfeiting [47]. In addition, Gao et al. combined N, S-CDs and Eu-MOF to form an N, S-CDs@Eu-MOF composite material. The combination of these two materials realized the encryption and decryption of the anti-counterfeiting mode, which has potential application value in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting technology [174].

Based on the above discussion, we know from many studies that N, S-CDs can be used as probes, biological imaging agents and drug carriers for multifunctional detection mechanisms. It has been successfully applied in many aspects of research and has broad prospects and application significance in protecting the environment, medical testing and food safety.

The co-doping of nitrogen and sulfur atoms gives carbon dots more excellent properties, setting them apart from single-doped carbon dots, which have attracted much attention from researchers in recent years. This makes nitrogen-sulfur co-doping an excellent new doping strategy. The primary characteristics of carbon dots with nitrogen and sulfur co-doping are a larger photoluminescence region and even tunable polychromatic emission (short to violet‒blue fluorescence and long wavelength to near-infrared region red light), higher water solubility, stronger high-light stability, a lower detection limit, better biocompatibility, etc. Along with its exceptional selectivity and fluorescence characteristics, these qualities also make it a good probe for food safety and a promising source of nanomaterials for biomedicine. In addition, the co-doping of nitrogen and sulfur results in the introduction of some functional groups on the surface of carbon dots, which further promotes the performance improvement of carbon dots and obtains co-doped carbon dots. Functionalized carbon materials such as electrochemical energy storage materials have great application potential.

Advancements based on a series of N, S-CDs are discussed in this review. We concentrate on N, S-CDs because of the diverse fabrication methods, variables affecting the fluorescence features, and prospective applications in a variety of disciplines. We also discuss the variables that may affect the fluorescence properties of N, S-CDs and their usefulness in environmental monitoring, biomedicine, and food safety. A wide range of synthetic strategies, ranging from different precursors to different synthetic methods, have been investigated. The characteristics of doped carbon dots can be improved even more by nitrogen-sulfur co-doping. By doping nitrogen and sulfur atoms into carbon dots and reducing the energy band gap, the luminescent properties of carbon dots can be tuned to achieve long wavelength emission or polychromatic emission. A large number of studies have found that different forms of nitrogen doping, such as pyridine nitrogen and pyrrolic nitrogen, play a key role in the red emission of doped carbon dots. At the same time, the electronegativity of sulfur atoms is lower than that of nitrogen, further narrowing the energy band gap of the doped carbon dots, and redshifting the photoluminescence wavelength redshift, so co-doping the carbon dots with sulfur and nitrogen atoms further improvesthe properties of the carbon dots. Long-wavelength emission can eliminate self-organizing interference factors to a certain extent, making it more practical in biomedicine. Additionally, studies have revealed a partial relationship between the carbon dot surface and the red emission of target-doped carbon dots. These are currently being researched regarding oxidation degrees. However, there are some issues with nitrogen-sulfur co-doping when studying carbon dots. Finally, we summarize the major challenges and issues of N, S-CDs as follows.
1)
First, achieving accurate and manageable nitrogen-sulfur co-doping still presents significant barriers. Furthermore, it is still challenging to establish quantitative control of nitrogen-sulfur atoms doped with carbon dots. For example, in addition to the doping content in the carbon dots, it is still a great challenge to find sites with more N and S doping in the carbon dots. If we can further explore the controllability of N,S-CDs in this aspect, it will be an important milestone in the research of N,S-CDs.
2)
Further study is required since the size management of N, S-CDs has not yet reached an acceptable level. Moreover, in the context of carbon neutrality, the toxicity of some nitrogen-sulfur co-doping carbon dots cannot be ruled out, and there are still some challenges in developing nontoxic nitrogen-sulfur co-doping.
3)
Finally, the preparation of nitrogen-sulfur co-doped carbon dots capable of both long-wavelength emission and mass production is still in the exploratory stage. According to research, the quantum yield of N, S-CDs emitted at long wavelengths is generally extremely low. If this shortcoming can be overcome, it will be a qualitative leap in the research of N, S-CDs.

In summary, with the deepening of research, nitrogen-sulfur co-doping of carbon dots can improve the performance of carbon dots to a certain extent and make up for the shortcomings of traditional carbon dots. These advantages are undoubted, and it still has broad prospects for multifield applications as luminescent nanomaterials. We believe that improvements in N, S-CDs performance are on the near horizon.
